content:
I am a Ph.D. candidate in Political Science at the University of Pittsburgh. My research focuses on the political economy of development and on statistical inference.
 I study the institutional origin of bureaucratic effectiveness and its effects on economic development in developing countries. My working papers examine this question through perspectives of state capture, bureaucratic incentives and performance, and their effects on economic growth and inequality.  Developing and improving statistical methods to facilitate researchers to answer their theoretical questions without relying on strict assumptions and expensive computational power. My working papers use machine learning methods to tackle issues in kernel regressions and heterogeneous effects in causal inference.  Before coming to the University of Pittsburgh, I received my Master degree in Political Science from Duke University and also graduated with a Master degree in Economics from the North Carolina State University.
You can reach me via email: qic47@pitt.edu. You can download my CV here.
summary:
I am a Ph.D. candidate in Political Science at the University of Pittsburgh. My research focuses on the political economy of development and on statistical inference.
 I study the institutional origin of bureaucratic effectiveness and its effects on economic development in developing countries.

content:
Course Description This course provides basic mathematical and statistical foundations for students to do more advanced formal theoretical and quantitative empirical research in political science. This class divides into three modules. The first module covers essential mathematical tools, such as calculus, optimization, and algebra. The second module introduces variety of statistical concept, probability theory, and logic of inference. The third module introduces potential outcome framework, ordinary least square, and maximum likelihood estimation, which put together all the knowledge learned from the first two modules.
This course emphasizes both conceptualization and application of all the mathematical and statistical tools. Therefore, the first part of the class focuses on concept and in-class exercise, and the second part focuses on how to implement algorithms and make large-scale calculations in the R statistical software.
Requirements Software Our main programming language for the purpose of this course will be the R programming language. Before our first meeting, you should install the following latest version software on your computer:
 R, you can download from here. RStudio Desktop Free version, you can download from here.  Textbooks There is only one required textbook for this class, the rest of them listed below are optional. Meanwhile, I will post all class handout to our course website.
 Required: Moore, Will H. and David A. Siegel. 2013. A Mathematics Course for Political & Social Research. Princeton University Press. Wackerly, Dennis. William Mendenhall, and Richard Scheaffer. 2008. Mathematical Statistics with Applications, 7th edition. Brooks/Cole Cengage Learning. Wickham, H. and Grolemund, G., 2016. R for Data Science: import, tidy, transform, visualize, and model data. O’Reilly.  Tentative Course Outline  Week 1 08/29 Introduction Week 2 09/12 Derivatives Week 3 09/19 Integrals Week 4 09/26 Intro to Probability Week 5 10/03 Random Variable 1 Week 6 10/10 MIDTERM EXAM Week 7 10/17 Random Variable 2 Week 8 10/24 Estimation and Inference 1 Week 9 10/31 Estimation and Inference 2 Week 10 11/07 Matrix Algebra 1 Week 11 11/14 Matrix Algebra 2 Week 12 11/21 Potential Outcome and Linear Regression Week 13 11/28 OLS Week 14 12/05 FINAL EXAM  You can download the full syllabus here
title: PS 2010:  Foundations of Quantitative Methods
PS 0700: Research Method for Political Science

content: Abstract: Existing literature demonstrates that local governments intentionally reduce industrial land prices to attract investment while increasing residential land prices to boost government revenue. This “development on land” strategy promotes economic growth but comes with the cost of increasingly inequality in rural areas. This paper provides systematically empirical evidence of how local governments’ intervention in residential land prices worsens rural inequality. I construct a dataset on rural inequality using nightlight density from satellite imagery. This approach allows me to measure inequality based on geographical boundary. Combining this dataset with land price data extracted from government digital archives, I show how residential land price distortion contributes substantively to rural inequality.
summary: This paper provides systematically empirical evidence of how local governments’ intervention in residential land prices worsens rural inequality.
title: Land Price Distortion and Rural Inequality

content: Abstract: How do political incentives influence the use of economic development incentives? This paper provides causal evidence on how politicians’ career incentives and competition among neighborhood jurisdictions affect frequently used development incentives: industrial and residential land. Using geographic regression discontinuity design and a spatial regression on millions of land transaction data and thousands of local political leaders’ career records, I find politicians’ career incentive leads to a decrease in both the industrial and residential land price whereas inter-jurisdiction competition leads to a decrease in industrial and an increase in the residential land price. In contrast to the large literature that does not separate the effects of career incentives and inter-jurisdiction competition, this paper demonstrates the importance of differentiating these two effects on identifying the effects of political incentives on development incentives.
summary: Politicians' career incentive leads to a decreases in the industrial and residential land price, whereas competition leads to a decrease in industrial and increase in residential land price in China.
title: Career Incentives, Inter-jurisdiction Competition, and Public Land Prices

content: Abstract: Information asymmetry is the core parameter in the principal agent model, but empirical studies tend to assume this by default and study its effect on outcomes of interest rather than measure information asymmetry explicitly. This paper develops a measure for information asymmetry and applies it to political selection in China to study how information asymmetry affects sub-national leaders’ promotion. I find that performance and patronage do matter in political promotion, but their effects depend on the intensity of information asymmetry: the higher the information asymmetry, the higher the effect of performance and connection on political promotion. However, a more detailed study reveals that performance and connection contributes differently to promotion. Specifically, performance does not contribute to promotion when information asymmetry is not severe but begins to affect promotion when information asymmetry is increasingly severe. In contrast, connection increases promotion when information asymmetry is not severe but does not affect promotion at all when information asymmetry is severe. These findings challenge the theoretical arguments that 1) political selection in China is either a meritocratic or patronage system, and 2) patronage is a complement of formal contract (institutions) when there is a severe information asymmetry.
summary: This paper develops a measure for information asymmetry and applies it to political selection in China to study how information asymmetry affects sub-national leaders' promotion.
title: Information Asymmetry in Political Selection: Performance versus Patronage
